title: Generative AI
blurb: ★ Overview only · Generative models learn a compact representation of their training data and sample new, similar content. This chapter covers the core generative architectures (GANs, VAEs, diffusion, Transformers), how LLMs are adapted through prompting, RAG and fine-tuning, and where generative AI is applied in EDA.

knowledge_points.title: Generative Model Architectures
knowledge_points.recap: **Generative AI** = deep learning models that generate new content (text, images, code) resembling their training data. **Foundation Models** are large networks trained with self-supervision on broad data and adapted to many downstream tasks. Key architectures: **GANs** (a generator and discriminator compete toward a Nash equilibrium), **VAEs** (an encoder compresses input into a latent space, a decoder reconstructs; new data is sampled from the latent space), **Diffusion models** (add Gaussian noise then learn to reverse the denoising), and the **Transformer** (self-attention, used for text-to-text).

questions.q: Match the generative mechanism to the model. Which statements are correct?
questions.answer: GAN = generator vs discriminator adversarial game; VAE = encode to latent space then decode; Diffusion = corrupt with Gaussian noise then learn to denoise.

options.text: A GAN trains a generator to produce fakes and a discriminator to tell fakes from real, competing toward a Nash equilibrium.
A VAE encodes input into a lower-dimensional latent space and decodes it back; new data is generated by sampling the latent space.
A diffusion model corrupts data with Gaussian noise (forward process) and learns to reverse it step-by-step (reverse process).
A GAN generates images by iteratively denoising pure Gaussian noise.

questions.q: Describe the forward and reverse processes of a diffusion model and how generation works after training.
questions.answer: A diffusion model works in two phases. In the **forward diffusion process** it gradually **corrupts training data by adding Gaussian noise** over many steps until the data becomes essentially pure noise. The network then learns the **reverse diffusion process**: how to **recover the original information by denoising step-by-step**. After training, generation starts from **random Gaussian noise** and passes it through the learned denoising process to produce a new sample. Common uses are text-to-image and image-to-image generation.
questions.extend: In EDA, chip placement has been framed as a diffusion problem that denoises component coordinates $x$ conditioned on the netlist $c$, placing all components simultaneously (unlike sequential RL placement).

questions.q: What is a foundation model, and list three challenges/limitations of generative AI.
questions.answer: A **foundation model** is a large-scale neural network trained with **self-supervision on vast amounts of broad, unlabeled data** that can be **adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks**. Challenges include: **hallucination / unreliable, untrustworthy outputs**; **data issues** (quality, bias, leaks, licensing); **large computational requirements**; **lack of domain-specific knowledge**; and **poor interpretability**, among others.

knowledge_points.title: Adapting LLMs: Prompting, RAG & Fine-tuning
knowledge_points.recap: LLMs struggle with **domain-specific** prompts and information outside their training data, and have a **knowledge cutoff** plus a tendency to **hallucinate**. Three adaptation techniques: **In-context learning / prompt engineering** (steer behavior with examples, no retraining), **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** (retrieve relevant chunks from an external knowledge base and condition generation on them, improving factuality and transparency), and **fine-tuning** (further train on a domain dataset). Use RAG for up-to-date, factual, transparent needs; use fine-tuning for a well-defined domain with a large high-quality dataset.

questions.q: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and what problem does it solve compared to a plain LLM? Give one advantage and one disadvantage.
questions.answer: **RAG** combines **retrieval** (fetching relevant information from an external knowledge base such as databases, docs or emails) with **generation** (the LLM produces an answer conditioned on the retrieved context and the user query). It addresses the LLM's **knowledge cutoff** and **hallucination** problems and lets the model answer questions about specific/up-to-date content it was never trained on, while being able to cite sources. An **advantage** is dynamic knowledge integration with improved accuracy/factuality and transparency; a **disadvantage** is dependency on the knowledge base plus added computational overhead and less control over generation style. Use RAG when facts and up-to-date knowledge matter; prefer fine-tuning when you have a well-defined domain with a large high-quality dataset and want to adapt style/tone.
questions.extend: In EDA, RAG-EDA and RTLFixer use custom RAG flows for tool-documentation Q&A and Verilog error fixing.

questions.q: **Fill in the blanks** (rotating variant each visit): the LangChain + Chroma + Instructor RAG pipeline over OpenROAD docs.
questions.answer:
The pipeline has these stages:

1. **Load** the source documents (OpenROAD Markdown files) with a `DirectoryLoader`.
2. **Chunk** them with a `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` (e.g. ~5000 chars, 50 char overlap) so retrieval is focused.
3. **Embed & store**: turn chunks into vectors with HuggingFace **Instructor embeddings** and persist them in a **Chroma** vector database.
```python
db = Chroma.from_documents(documents=chunks, embedding=embedding,
                           persist_directory=str(CHROMA_PATH))
```
4. **Retrieve**: for a user query, run a similarity search to return the top-k most relevant chunks.
```python
matches = db.similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(query, k=3, score_threshold=0.7)
```
5. **Generate**: stuff the retrieved chunks into a prompt template as *context* alongside the question and pass it to an LLM, then display the answer with its **sources** for transparency.

Embeddings place semantically similar text close together (cosine/Euclidean distance), which is what makes relevant retrieval possible.
questions.extend: The notebook illustrates context-dependent embeddings: 'apple' is closer to 'iPhone' than to 'orange' when interpreted as the company, showing embeddings capture meaning.

blanks.hint: build a Chroma store from chunks

variants.code:
```python
splitter = ____(1)(chunk_size=5000, chunk_overlap=50)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
matches = db.____(2)(query, ____(3)=3, score_threshold=0.7)
```

blanks.hint: how many top chunks to return

variants.code:
```python
# 1. Load docs with DirectoryLoader
# 2. ____(1) with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
# 3. ____(2) chunks with Instructor embeddings into Chroma
# 4. ____(3) top-k relevant chunks for the query
# 5. Generate answer from a prompt template + context
```

questions.q: When is fine-tuning preferable to RAG for adapting an LLM?
questions.answer: When you have a well-defined domain with a large, high-quality dataset and want to improve a specific task or match writing style/tone.

options.text: You have a well-defined domain and a large, high-quality domain-specific dataset, and want to specialize the model's task performance and style.
Your task mainly needs up-to-the-minute factual information from a constantly changing source.
Transparency and citing the exact source document are the top priority.
You want to adapt to a new task with zero additional training using only examples in the prompt.

questions.extend: Fine-tuning risks overfitting and catastrophic forgetting and is expensive; in-context learning adapts quickly with no retraining but may be less accurate than RAG or a fine-tuned model.

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style concept questions in the professor's format. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: Compare the generative mechanisms of a **GAN**, a **VAE** and a **diffusion model**. For each, describe in one or two sentences how it is trained and how new samples are generated.
questions.answer: **GAN:** a **generator** and a **discriminator** are trained adversarially — the generator produces fakes, the discriminator learns to tell fake from real, and they compete toward a Nash equilibrium; generation feeds random noise through the trained generator. **VAE:** an **encoder** compresses input into a lower-dimensional **latent space** and a **decoder** reconstructs it; new data is generated by **sampling the latent space** and decoding. **Diffusion model:** a **forward process** gradually corrupts data with **Gaussian noise**, and the network learns the **reverse (denoising) process**; generation starts from pure random noise and iteratively denoises it into a sample.
questions.extend: In EDA, chip placement has been framed as a diffusion problem that denoises component coordinates conditioned on the netlist, placing all components simultaneously rather than sequentially like RL.

questions.q: (a) What problems of a plain LLM does **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** address? (b) When would you prefer **fine-tuning** over RAG? Give the main deciding factors.
questions.answer: (a) A plain LLM has a **knowledge cutoff**, lacks **domain-specific/private** information it was never trained on, and tends to **hallucinate**. **RAG** retrieves relevant chunks from an **external knowledge base** and conditions generation on them, so answers can be **up-to-date, factual and transparent** (it can cite sources) without retraining. (b) Prefer **fine-tuning** when you have a **well-defined domain with a large, high-quality dataset** and want to **specialize task performance or match a style/tone**. RAG is better when facts, freshness and source transparency matter; fine-tuning is better for deep domain specialization but risks overfitting/catastrophic forgetting and is expensive.
questions.extend: In-context learning/prompt engineering is a third, lightweight option: it steers behavior with in-prompt examples and no retraining. EDA examples: RAG-EDA and RTLFixer use custom RAG flows.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about foundation models and LLM adaptation are correct?
questions.answer: Foundation models are self-supervised on broad data and adapted downstream; RAG grounds answers in retrieved context to reduce hallucination; prompting needs no retraining; hallucination is a real limitation.

options.text: A foundation model is trained with self-supervision on broad, largely unlabeled data and adapted to many downstream tasks.
RAG conditions the LLM on retrieved external context, improving factuality and allowing source citation.
In-context learning / prompt engineering adapts an LLM's behavior with no additional training.
Fine-tuning is always the best choice when information changes minute-to-minute and sources must be cited.

questions.extend: Generative AI limitations also include hallucination, data bias/licensing issues, high compute cost and poor interpretability.
